1→# Architecture Documentation
     2→
     3→**Purpose:** Explains technical decisions, technology choices, and architectural approaches. For implementation examples, see [PATTERNS.md](./PATTERNS.md).
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Technology Stack
     8→
     9→### Core Framework
    10→
    11→- **React 19** - UI library with modern features (no explicit import needed for JSX)
    12→- **TypeScript** - Type-safe JavaScript with strict mode enabled
    13→- **Vite** - Fast build tool and dev server
    14→
    15→### State Management
    16→
    17→- **@preact/signals-react** - Reactive global state management
    18→- **@tanstack/react-query (v5)** - Server state management and caching
    19→- **react-hook-form** - Form state and validation
    20→
    21→### Routing
    22→
    23→- **React Router v7** - Client-side routing with code splitting
    24→
    25→### HTTP & API
    26→
    27→- **axios** - HTTP client with interceptors
    28→- **Custom requestClient** - Wrapper around axios for API calls
    29→
    30→### UI & Styling
    31→
    32→- **SCSS** - CSS preprocessor for component styling
    33→- **classnames** - Conditional CSS class composition
    34→
    35→### Error Tracking
    36→
    37→- **@sentry/react** - Error monitoring and tracking
    38→
    39→### Testing
    40→
    41→- **Vitest** - Unit test runner
    42→- **@testing-library/react** - Component testing utilities
    43→- **Playwright** - End-to-end testing
    44→
    45→### Development Tools
    46→
    47→- **ESLint** - Code linting
    48→- **TypeScript** - Type checking
    49→- **universal-cookie** - Cookie management
    50→
    51→---
    52→
    53→## Application Architecture
    54→
    55→### High-Level Structure
    56→
    57→```
    58→┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    59→│              React Application              │
    60→├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    61→│  Presentation Layer (Components/Pages)      │
    62→│  ├─ Form Components (AuButton, AuField)    │
    63→│  ├─ Layout Components (MainLayout)          │
    64→│  ├─ Page Components (TransactionsPage)      │
    65→│  └─ Reusable Components (Modal, Sidebar)   │
    66→├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    67→│  Business Logic Layer (Services)            │
    68→│  ├─ authService (Authentication)            │
    69→│  ├─ modalService (UI State)                 │
    70→│  ├─ sidebarService (UI State)               │
    71→│  ├─ notificationService (Alerts)            │
    72→│  └─ aiAssistantService (AI Chat)            │
    73→├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    74→│  Data Layer (Repositories)                  │
    75→│  ├─ useTransactionList (Queries)            │
    76→│  ├─ useCreateTransaction (Mutations)        │
    77→│  ├─ useLoadBudgets (Queries)                │
    78→│  └─ React Query Cache                       │
    79→├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    80→│  API Layer (Request Client)                 │
    81→│  ├─ axios (HTTP Client)                     │
    82→│  ├─ requestClient (Wrapper)                 │
    83→│  └─ API Gateway Enum (Endpoints)            │
    84→├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    85→│  Storage Layer                              │
    86→│  ├─ localStorageService (Persistent)        │
    87→│  ├─ cookieService (Persistent)              │
    88→│  └─ globalStore (In-Memory)                 │
    89→└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    90→                     ↓
    91→          ┌──────────────────┐
    92→          │  Backend REST API │
    93→          └──────────────────┘
    94→```
    95→
    96→**Why this layered approach:**
    97→
    98→- Clear separation of concerns
    99→- Easy to test each layer independently
   100→- Business logic isolated from UI
   101→- Data fetching abstracted from components
   102→- State management separated from data fetching
   103→
   104→---
   105→
   106→## State Management Architecture
   107→
   108→### Decision: Three-State Approach
   109→
   110→We use **three different state management solutions** for different purposes:
   111→
   112→1. **Global UI State** → @preact/signals
   113→2. **Server State** → @tanstack/react-query
   114→3. **Form State** → react-hook-form
   115→
   116→**Why not a single solution?**
   117→
   118→- Each tool is optimized for its specific use case
   119→- Smaller bundle size than a monolithic solution
   120→- Better performance for each scenario
   121→- Industry best practices
   122→
   123→---
   124→
   125→### 1. Global State (@preact/signals)
   126→
   127→**Purpose:** Manage UI state that needs to be accessed across the entire application.
   128→
   129→**Location:** `src/shared/store/globalStore.ts`
   130→
   131→**What we store:**
   132→
   133→- Auth token
   134→- Notifications
   135→- Modal stack
   136→- Sidebar stack
   137→- Optimistic update ID lists
   138→
   139→**Why @preact/signals over Context API?**
   140→
   141→- **Performance:** Fine-grained updates, only subscribed components re-render
   142→- **No Provider Hell:** Direct access to store, no nested providers
   143→- **Size:** ~1KB vs Context API overhead
   144→- **Developer Experience:** Simple API, no boilerplate
   145→- **Framework Agnostic:** Core is not React-specific
   146→
   147→**Why not Redux/Zustand?**
   148→
   149→- Signals are faster (fine-grained reactivity)
   150→- Less boilerplate than Redux
   151→- More lightweight than Zustand
   152→- We don't need time-travel debugging or middleware
   153→
   154→**Access Pattern:**
   155→
   156→- Components: `useGlobalValue(field)` hook
   157→- Services: `createGlobalValueSetter(field)` for updates
   158→
   159→See [PATTERNS.md#3-state-management-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#3-state-management-pattern) for implementation examples.
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→### 2. Server State (@tanstack/react-query)
   164→
   165→**Purpose:** Manage server data with automatic caching, refetching, and synchronization.
   166→
   167→**Location:** `src/shared/requestClient/queryClient.ts`
   168→
   169→**Configuration Philosophy:**
   170→
   171→```
   172→refetchOnWindowFocus: false  // Don't refetch on window focus (avoids unnecessary requests)
   173→retry: 1                     // Retry failed requests once (fail fast)
   174→staleTime: 5 minutes         // Data fresh for 5 minutes (balance between fresh and cached)
   175→throwOnError: true           // Throw errors to boundaries (consistent error handling)
   176→```
   177→
   178→**Why React Query over Redux?**
   179→
   180→- **Purpose-built:** Designed specifically for async server state
   181→- **Less Boilerplate:** No actions, reducers, sagas
   182→- **Auto Caching:** Handles request deduplication, background refetching
   183→- **Optimistic Updates:** Built-in support
   184→- **DevTools:** Excellent debugging tools for async data
   185→- **Industry Standard:** Most popular solution for server state
   186→
   187→**Why not fetch directly in components?**
   188→
   189→- Manual cache management is complex
   190→- No automatic refetching
   191→- No request deduplication
   192→- Hard to handle loading/error states consistently
   193→
   194→**Query Key Convention:**
   195→
   196→```
   197→[ApiGatewayE.ENDPOINT, ...filters/params]
   198→```
   199→
   200→This ensures proper cache invalidation and prevents cache collisions.
   201→
   202→See [PATTERNS.md#4-data-fetching-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#4-data-fetching-pattern) for implementation examples.
   203→
   204→---
   205→
   206→### 3. Form State (react-hook-form)
   207→
   208→**Purpose:** Manage form inputs, validation, and submission.
   209→
   210→**Why react-hook-form over Formik?**
   211→
   212→- **Performance:** Uncontrolled inputs, no re-render on every keystroke
   213→- **Bundle Size:** Smaller footprint (~9KB vs 15KB)
   214→- **TypeScript:** Better type inference
   215→- **Flexibility:** Works with any input library
   216→- **Validation:** Built-in + schema validation support
   217→
   218→**Why not controlled inputs?**
   219→
   220→- Controlled inputs re-render on every keystroke
   221→- Poor performance for large forms
   222→- Unnecessary React updates
   223→
   224→**Key Design:**
   225→
   226→- Uncontrolled by default (refs instead of state)
   227→- Validation on blur (not on change)
   228→- Server errors mapped to fields
   229→- Auto-focus first error
   230→
   231→See [ARCHITECTURE.md#form-handling-architecture](#form-handling-architecture) for details.
   232→
   233→---
   234→
   235→## API Communication Architecture
   236→
   237→### Request Client Pattern
   238→
   239→**Location:** `src/shared/requestClient/requestClient.ts`
   240→
   241→**Why a wrapper around axios?**
   242→
   243→- **Consistent API:** Same interface for all requests
   244→- **Type Safety:** Generic responses `<R>`
   245→- **Error Handling:** Centralized error transformation
   246→- **URL Building:** Automatic path and query param handling
   247→- **Unwrapping:** Returns `response.data` directly
   248→
   249→**Why not use fetch?**
   250→
   251→- Axios has interceptors (global request/response handling)
   252→- Built-in timeout support
   253→- Automatic error rejection on non-2xx
   254→- Better browser support with polyfills
   255→
   256→---
   257→
   258→### API Gateway Enum
   259→
   260→**Location:** `src/shared/types/api/ApiGatewayE.ts`
   261→
   262→**Why an enum for endpoints?**
   263→
   264→- **Single Source of Truth:** Change once, update everywhere
   265→- **TypeScript Autocomplete:** No typos in endpoint strings
   266→- **Refactoring:** Find all usages easily
   267→- **Type Safety:** Used as query keys in React Query
   268→
   269→**Pattern:**
   270→
   271→```typescript
   272→export enum ApiGatewayE {
   273→  BANK_TRANSACTIONS = '/api/bank-transactions',
   274→  BANK_TRANSACTIONS_DETAILS = '/api/bank-transactions/:id',
   275→}
   276→```
   277→
   278→---
   279→
   280→### Axios Interceptors
   281→
   282→**Why interceptors?**
   283→
   284→- **Auth Token Injection:** Automatic on every request
   285→- **Global Error Handling:** 401 redirects to login
   286→- **Request/Response Logging:** Development debugging
   287→- **Error Transformation:** User-friendly messages
   288→
   289→**Request Interceptor:**
   290→
   291→- Adds `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header automatically
   292→- No need to add token manually in every API call
   293→
   294→**Response Interceptor:**
   295→
   296→- Handles 401 (unauthorized) globally → logout and redirect
   297→- Prevents duplicated error handling in every component
   298→
   299→---
   300→
   301→## Routing Architecture
   302→
   303→### React Router v7 Setup
   304→
   305→**Location:** `src/app/routes/AppRoutes.tsx`
   306→
   307→**Why React Router v7?**
   308→
   309→- Industry standard
   310→- Code splitting support
   311→- Nested routes
   312→- Type-safe route params
   313→- Excellent TypeScript support
   314→
   315→**Route Guards Pattern:**
   316→
   317→- `OnlyPrivate` - Protected routes (requires auth)
   318→- `OnlyPublic` - Public routes (redirects if authenticated)
   319→
   320→**Why route guards?**
   321→
   322→- Centralized auth logic
   323→- Prevents code duplication
   324→- Easy to add new protected routes
   325→- Clear route organization
   326→
   327→---
   328→
   329→### Sentry Integration
   330→
   331→```typescript
   332→const sentryCreateBrowserRouter =
   333→  Sentry.wrapCreateBrowserRouterV6(createBrowserRouter);
   334→```
   335→
   336→**Why wrap router?**
   337→
   338→- Automatic error tracking on route changes
   339→- Performance monitoring per route
   340→- User session recording
   341→- Breadcrumb trail for debugging
   342→
   343→---
   344→
   345→### Path Constants
   346→
   347→**Location:** `src/shared/constants/appPaths.ts`
   348→
   349→**Why `AppPath` class?**
   350→
   351→- **Type-Safe Parameters:** Compile-time checks for required params
   352→- **Single Source of Truth:** Change path once
   353→- **Easy Refactoring:** Find all usages
   354→- **Prevents Typos:** No hardcoded strings
   355→
   356→**Pattern:**
   357→
   358→```typescript
   359→export const appPaths = {
   360→  BUDGET: new AppPath('/budgets/:budgetId'),
   361→};
   362→```
   363→
   364→See [PATTERNS.md#6-routing-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#6-routing-pattern) for usage examples.
   365→
   366→---
   367→
   368→## Repository Pattern (Data Layer)
   369→
   370→**Purpose:** Encapsulate API calls and React Query logic.
   371→
   372→**Why repository pattern?**
   373→
   374→- **Separation of Concerns:** UI doesn't know about API details
   375→- **Reusability:** One hook used by many components
   376→- **Testability:** Mock repositories instead of API
   377→- **Type Safety:** Typed responses
   378→- **Consistency:** Standard naming and error handling
   379→
   380→**Naming Convention:**
   381→
   382→- `useLoadX` - Queries (read)
   383→- `useCreateX` - Mutations (create)
   384→- `useEditX` - Mutations (update)
   385→- `useDeleteX` - Mutations (delete)
   386→
   387→**Why this naming?**
   388→
   389→- Clear intent (load vs create vs edit)
   390→- Consistent across codebase
   391→- Easy to search
   392→- Aligns with HTTP methods
   393→
   394→See [PATTERNS.md#4-data-fetching-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#4-data-fetching-pattern) for implementation examples.
   395→
   396→---
   397→
   398→## Modal & Sidebar Architecture
   399→
   400→### Service-Managed UI Pattern
   401→
   402→**Concept:** UI state (modals, sidebars) managed by services, not component state.
   403→
   404→**Why service-managed?**
   405→
   406→- **Open from Anywhere:** Even outside React (services, utilities)
   407→- **Centralized State:** One source of truth
   408→- **Type Safety:** Props validated at compile time
   409→- **Animation Support:** State-driven animations
   410→- **Stacking Support:** Multiple sidebars open simultaneously
   411→
   412→**Why not component state?**
   413→
   414→- Can't open modal from service/utility
   415→- Hard to share state between components
   416→- Each modal needs own state management
   417→- No type safety for props
   418→
   419→**Type Safety via Mapped Types:**
   420→
   421→```typescript
   422→export type SidebarPropsT = {
   423→  [SidebarNameE.EDIT_BUDGET]: { budgetId: UuidT };
   424→  [SidebarNameE.CREATE_TRANSACTION]: void;
   425→};
   426→```
   427→
   428→TypeScript ensures correct props for each sidebar at compile time.
   429→
   430→See [PATTERNS.md#1-modal-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#1-modal-pattern) and [PATTERNS.md#2-sidebar-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#2-sidebar-pattern) for usage examples.
   431→
   432→---
   433→
   434→## Error Handling Architecture
   435→
   436→### Multi-Layer Error Handling
   437→
   438→**Philosophy:** Handle errors at the appropriate layer, fail gracefully.
   439→
   440→**Layers:**
   441→
   442→1. **Global Error Boundary** - Catches uncaught errors, shows fallback UI
   443→2. **Route Error Boundary** - Catches routing errors, shows error page
   444→3. **React Query** - Throws errors to boundaries (`throwOnError: true`)
   445→4. **Form Validation** - Field-level errors, prevent submission
   446→5. **Service Layer** - Transform errors, user-friendly messages
   447→
   448→**Why multi-layer?**
   449→
   450→- Different error types need different handling
   451→- User sees appropriate feedback
   452→- Errors don't crash entire app
   453→- All errors tracked in Sentry
   454→
   455→**Error Flow:**
   456→
   457→```
   458→API Error
   459→  ↓
   460→Service catches & transforms
   461→  ↓
   462→Repository re-throws
   463→  ↓
   464→Component catches OR Error Boundary catches
   465→  ↓
   466→Display to user (notification or error page)
   467→  ↓
   468→Report to Sentry (if uncaught)
   469→```
   470→
   471→---
   472→
   473→## Authentication Architecture
   474→
   475→### Flow
   476→
   477→```
   478→1. User enters email → POST /api/auth/login
   479→   ↓
   480→2. Server sends 6-digit code to email
   481→   ↓
   482→3. User enters code → POST /api/auth/verify
   483→   ↓
   484→4. Server returns JWT token
   485→   ↓
   486→5. authService.login(token)
   487→   ├─ Store in localStorage
   488→   ├─ Update global state
   489→   └─ Set Sentry user
   490→   ↓
   491→6. Navigate to app
   492→   ↓
   493→7. Axios interceptor adds token to all requests
   494→   ↓
   495→8. On 401 error → authService.logout() → Navigate to /login
   496→```
   497→
   498→**Why JWT?**
   499→
   500→- Stateless (no server-side session storage)
   501→- Can be validated independently
   502→- Contains user info (for Sentry tracking)
   503→- Industry standard
   504→
   505→**Why localStorage?**
   506→
   507→- Persists across browser sessions
   508→- Simple API
   509→- Synchronous (no async needed)
   510→
   511→**Why not cookies?**
   512→
   513→- More complex (CSRF protection needed)
   514→- Size limitations
   515→- Cookie policy restrictions
   516→
   517→**Token Injection:**
   518→
   519→- Axios request interceptor adds token automatically
   520→- No need to add manually in every request
   521→
   522→**Token Invalidation:**
   523→
   524→- On 401 response, axios interceptor triggers logout
   525→- Clears localStorage, global state, React Query cache
   526→- Redirects to login page
   527→
   528→---
   529→
   530→## Form Handling Architecture
   531→
   532→### AuForm Component
   533→
   534→**Why custom form wrapper?**
   535→
   536→- **Consistent API:** All forms work the same
   537→- **Server Validation:** Automatic error mapping
   538→- **Auto-Focus:** First error field gets focus
   539→- **Enter Key Handling:** Submit on Enter (last input)
   540→- **Error Display:** Standardized error UI
   541→
   542→**Validation Error Flow:**
   543→
   544→```
   545→1. Form submits → API call
   546→   ↓
   547→2. Server returns 400 with ValidationError
   548→   ↓
   549→3. AuForm catches ValidationError
   550→   ↓
   551→4. Maps field errors to form fields
   552→   ↓
   553→5. Displays errors below inputs
   554→   ↓
   555→6. Focuses first error field
   556→```
   557→
   558→**Why this approach?**
   559→
   560→- Client and server validation aligned
   561→- User sees errors next to relevant field
   562→- Keyboard navigation works (auto-focus)
   563→- Consistent UX across all forms
   564→
   565→---
   566→
   567→## Performance Optimizations
   568→
   569→### 1. Code Splitting
   570→
   571→- **Route-based:** Each page lazy loaded
   572→- **Dynamic imports:** Heavy components on demand
   573→- **Vite handles:** Automatic chunk splitting
   574→
   575→### 2. React Query Caching
   576→
   577→- **5-minute stale time:** Avoid redundant requests
   578→- **Background refetching:** Always fresh data
   579→- **Optimistic updates:** Instant UI feedback
   580→
   581→### 3. Signals (Fine-Grained Reactivity)
   582→
   583→- **Only subscribed components re-render**
   584→- **No Context provider overhead**
   585→- **Minimal wasted renders**
   586→
   587→### 4. Form Optimization
   588→
   589→- **Uncontrolled inputs:** No re-render on keystroke
   590→- **Validation on blur:** Not on every change
   591→- **Debounced validation:** For async checks
   592→
   593→### 5. Memoization
   594→
   595→- **React.memo:** For expensive components
   596→- **useMemo:** For expensive calculations
   597→- **useCallback:** For stable function references
   598→
   599→---
   600→
   601→## Development Tools
   602→
   603→### Vite Configuration
   604→
   605→**Why Vite over Create React App?**
   606→
   607→- **Faster:** ES modules, no bundling in dev
   608→- **Smaller:** Optimized production builds
   609→- **Modern:** Built for modern browsers
   610→- **Flexible:** Easy to configure
   611→
   612→**Features:**
   613→
   614→- HMR (Hot Module Replacement)
   615→- Fast refresh for React
   616→- Proxy to backend at `/api`
   617→- Tree-shaking and minification
   618→- Source maps for Sentry
   619→
   620→### TypeScript Configuration
   621→
   622→**Strict Mode Enabled:**
   623→
   624→- Catches more errors at compile time
   625→- Forces explicit types
   626→- Prevents `any` proliferation
   627→
   628→**Path Aliases:**
   629→
   630→- Configured in `tsconfig.json` and `vite.config.ts`
   631→- Must be kept in sync
   632→- Makes imports cleaner
   633→
   634→See [PATTERNS.md#7-path-aliases](./PATTERNS.md#7-path-aliases) for details.
   635→
   636→### Sentry Integration
   637→
   638→**Why Sentry?**
   639→
   640→- **Error Tracking:** Catch production errors
   641→- **Source Maps:** Readable stack traces
   642→- **User Context:** Know who experienced error
   643→- **Performance Monitoring:** Track slow routes
   644→- **Session Replay:** See what user did before error
   645→
   646→**Configuration:**
   647→
   648→- DSN from environment variable
   649→- Router instrumentation for navigation tracking
   650→- 10% traces sample rate (balance cost/coverage)
   651→
   652→---
   653→
   654→## Testing Architecture
   655→
   656→### Why These Tools?
   657→
   658→**Vitest over Jest:**
   659→
   660→- Faster (native ESM support)
   661→- Better Vite integration
   662→- Compatible with Jest API
   663→- Modern, actively maintained
   664→
   665→**@testing-library/react:**
   666→
   667→- Test user behavior, not implementation
   668→- Encourages accessible components
   669→- Query by role, label, text (not class names)
   670→- Industry standard
   671→
   672→**Playwright over Cypress:**
   673→
   674→- True cross-browser testing
   675→- Better TypeScript support
   676→- Faster execution
   677→- Built-in auto-waiting
   678→
   679→### What to Test Where
   680→
   681→**Unit Tests (Vitest):**
   682→
   683→- Business logic in services
   684→- Complex calculations
   685→- Validation functions
   686→- Edge cases
   687→
   688→**Component Tests (@testing-library):**
   689→
   690→- User interactions
   691→- Conditional rendering
   692→- Form validation
   693→- Error states
   694→
   695→**E2E Tests (Playwright):**
   696→
   697→- Critical user flows
   698→- Multi-page journeys
   699→- Real browser behavior
   700→- Integration between features
   701→
   702→**What NOT to Test:**
   703→
   704→- Simple prop passing
   705→- Framework behavior
   706→- Trivial assignments
   707→- Third-party libraries
   708→
   709→---
   710→
   711→## Key Architectural Decisions
   712→
   713→### Why @preact/signals over Context API?
   714→
   715→✅ Fine-grained updates, no provider overhead, tiny bundle
   716→
   717→### Why React Query over Redux?
   718→
   719→✅ Purpose-built for server state, less boilerplate, auto caching
   720→
   721→### Why react-hook-form over Formik?
   722→
   723→✅ Better performance (uncontrolled), smaller bundle, better TypeScript
   724→
   725→### Why Axios over Fetch?
   726→
   727→✅ Interceptors, timeouts, automatic error handling, better browser support
   728→
   729→### Why SCSS over CSS-in-JS?
   730→
   731→✅ No runtime cost, standard CSS syntax, better tooling, clear separation
   732→
   733→### Why Service-Managed UI (modals/sidebars)?
   734→
   735→✅ Type-safe props, open from anywhere, animation support, centralized state
   736→
   737→### Why Repository Pattern?
   738→
   739→✅ Separation of concerns, reusable, testable, consistent API
   740→
   741→### Why Route Guards?
   742→
   743→✅ Centralized auth logic, no duplication, clear route organization
   744→
   745→---
   746→
   747→## Future Architecture Considerations
   748→
   749→### Potential Improvements
   750→
   751→1. **State Management Evolution:**
   752→
   753→   - Monitor signals performance at scale
   754→   - Consider Zustand if complexity grows
   755→
   756→2. **Data Fetching:**
   757→
   758→   - Explore GraphQL for flexible querying
   759→   - WebSockets/SSE for real-time updates
   760→
   761→3. **Performance:**
   762→
   763→   - Service Workers for offline support
   764→   - Web Workers for heavy computations
   765→   - Virtual scrolling for large lists
   766→
   767→4. **Modularity:**
   768→
   769→   - Micro-frontends if team scales
   770→   - Feature flags for gradual rollouts
   771→
   772→5. **Monitoring:**
   773→   - Real User Monitoring (RUM)
   774→   - Web Vitals tracking
   775→   - Custom performance metrics
   776→
   777→---
   778→
   779→## For Agents
   780→
   781→When making architectural decisions:
   782→
   783→1. **Follow established patterns** - Don't introduce new approaches without reason
   784→2. **Check this doc first** - Understand why choices were made
   785→3. **Consistency over cleverness** - Use existing solutions
   786→4. **Performance matters** - Consider bundle size and runtime cost
   787→5. **Type safety is key** - Leverage TypeScript fully
   788→
   789→If you need to deviate from established architecture, document why in your output.
   790→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
# Architecture Documentation

**Purpose:** Explains technical decisions, technology choices, and architectural approaches. For implementation examples, see [PATTERNS.md](./PATTERNS.md).

---

## Technology Stack

### Core Framework

- **React 19** - UI library with modern features (no explicit import needed for JSX)
- **TypeScript** - Type-safe JavaScript with strict mode enabled
- **Vite** - Fast build tool and dev server

### State Management

- **@preact/signals-react** - Reactive global state management
- **@tanstack/react-query (v5)** - Server state management and caching
- **react-hook-form** - Form state and validation

### Routing

- **React Router v7** - Client-side routing with code splitting

### HTTP & API

- **axios** - HTTP client with interceptors
- **Custom requestClient** - Wrapper around axios for API calls

### UI & Styling

- **SCSS** - CSS preprocessor for component styling
- **classnames** - Conditional CSS class composition

### Error Tracking

- **@sentry/react** - Error monitoring and tracking

### Testing

- **Vitest** - Unit test runner
- **@testing-library/react** - Component testing utilities
- **Playwright** - End-to-end testing

### Development Tools

- **ESLint** - Code linting
- **TypeScript** - Type checking
- **universal-cookie** - Cookie management

---

## Application Architecture

### High-Level Structure

```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              React Application              │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Presentation Layer (Components/Pages)      │
│  ├─ Form Components (AuButton, AuField)    │
│  ├─ Layout Components (MainLayout)          │
│  ├─ Page Components (TransactionsPage)      │
│  └─ Reusable Components (Modal, Sidebar)   │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Business Logic Layer (Services)            │
│  ├─ authService (Authentication)            │
│  ├─ modalService (UI State)                 │
│  ├─ sidebarService (UI State)               │
│  ├─ notificationService (Alerts)            │
│  └─ aiAssistantService (AI Chat)            │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Data Layer (Repositories)                  │
│  ├─ useTransactionList (Queries)            │
│  ├─ useCreateTransaction (Mutations)        │
│  ├─ useLoadBudgets (Queries)                │
│  └─ React Query Cache                       │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  API Layer (Request Client)                 │
│  ├─ axios (HTTP Client)                     │
│  ├─ requestClient (Wrapper)                 │
│  └─ API Gateway Enum (Endpoints)            │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Storage Layer                              │
│  ├─ localStorageService (Persistent)        │
│  ├─ cookieService (Persistent)              │
│  └─ globalStore (In-Memory)                 │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                     ↓
          ┌──────────────────┐
          │  Backend REST API │
          └──────────────────┘
```

**Why this layered approach:**

- Clear separation of concerns
- Easy to test each layer independently
- Business logic isolated from UI
- Data fetching abstracted from components
- State management separated from data fetching

---

## State Management Architecture

### Decision: Three-State Approach

We use **three different state management solutions** for different purposes:

1. **Global UI State** → @preact/signals
2. **Server State** → @tanstack/react-query
3. **Form State** → react-hook-form

**Why not a single solution?**

- Each tool is optimized for its specific use case
- Smaller bundle size than a monolithic solution
- Better performance for each scenario
- Industry best practices

---

### 1. Global State (@preact/signals)

**Purpose:** Manage UI state that needs to be accessed across the entire application.

**Location:** `src/shared/store/globalStore.ts`

**What we store:**

- Auth token
- Notifications
- Modal stack
- Sidebar stack
- Optimistic update ID lists

**Why @preact/signals over Context API?**

- **Performance:** Fine-grained updates, only subscribed components re-render
- **No Provider Hell:** Direct access to store, no nested providers
- **Size:** ~1KB vs Context API overhead
- **Developer Experience:** Simple API, no boilerplate
- **Framework Agnostic:** Core is not React-specific

**Why not Redux/Zustand?**

- Signals are faster (fine-grained reactivity)
- Less boilerplate than Redux
- More lightweight than Zustand
- We don't need time-travel debugging or middleware

**Access Pattern:**

- Components: `useGlobalValue(field)` hook
- Services: `createGlobalValueSetter(field)` for updates

See [PATTERNS.md#3-state-management-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#3-state-management-pattern) for implementation examples.

---

### 2. Server State (@tanstack/react-query)

**Purpose:** Manage server data with automatic caching, refetching, and synchronization.

**Location:** `src/shared/requestClient/queryClient.ts`

**Configuration Philosophy:**

```
refetchOnWindowFocus: false  // Don't refetch on window focus (avoids unnecessary requests)
retry: 1                     // Retry failed requests once (fail fast)
staleTime: 5 minutes         // Data fresh for 5 minutes (balance between fresh and cached)
throwOnError: true           // Throw errors to boundaries (consistent error handling)
```

**Why React Query over Redux?**

- **Purpose-built:** Designed specifically for async server state
- **Less Boilerplate:** No actions, reducers, sagas
- **Auto Caching:** Handles request deduplication, background refetching
- **Optimistic Updates:** Built-in support
- **DevTools:** Excellent debugging tools for async data
- **Industry Standard:** Most popular solution for server state

**Why not fetch directly in components?**

- Manual cache management is complex
- No automatic refetching
- No request deduplication
- Hard to handle loading/error states consistently

**Query Key Convention:**

```
[ApiGatewayE.ENDPOINT, ...filters/params]
```

This ensures proper cache invalidation and prevents cache collisions.

See [PATTERNS.md#4-data-fetching-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#4-data-fetching-pattern) for implementation examples.

---

### 3. Form State (react-hook-form)

**Purpose:** Manage form inputs, validation, and submission.

**Why react-hook-form over Formik?**

- **Performance:** Uncontrolled inputs, no re-render on every keystroke
- **Bundle Size:** Smaller footprint (~9KB vs 15KB)
- **TypeScript:** Better type inference
- **Flexibility:** Works with any input library
- **Validation:** Built-in + schema validation support

**Why not controlled inputs?**

- Controlled inputs re-render on every keystroke
- Poor performance for large forms
- Unnecessary React updates

**Key Design:**

- Uncontrolled by default (refs instead of state)
- Validation on blur (not on change)
- Server errors mapped to fields
- Auto-focus first error

See [ARCHITECTURE.md#form-handling-architecture](#form-handling-architecture) for details.

---

## API Communication Architecture

### Request Client Pattern

**Location:** `src/shared/requestClient/requestClient.ts`

**Why a wrapper around axios?**

- **Consistent API:** Same interface for all requests
- **Type Safety:** Generic responses `<R>`
- **Error Handling:** Centralized error transformation
- **URL Building:** Automatic path and query param handling
- **Unwrapping:** Returns `response.data` directly

**Why not use fetch?**

- Axios has interceptors (global request/response handling)
- Built-in timeout support
- Automatic error rejection on non-2xx
- Better browser support with polyfills

---

### API Gateway Enum

**Location:** `src/shared/types/api/ApiGatewayE.ts`

**Why an enum for endpoints?**

- **Single Source of Truth:** Change once, update everywhere
- **TypeScript Autocomplete:** No typos in endpoint strings
- **Refactoring:** Find all usages easily
- **Type Safety:** Used as query keys in React Query

**Pattern:**

```typescript
export enum ApiGatewayE {
  BANK_TRANSACTIONS = '/api/bank-transactions',
  BANK_TRANSACTIONS_DETAILS = '/api/bank-transactions/:id',
}
```

---

### Axios Interceptors

**Why interceptors?**

- **Auth Token Injection:** Automatic on every request
- **Global Error Handling:** 401 redirects to login
- **Request/Response Logging:** Development debugging
- **Error Transformation:** User-friendly messages

**Request Interceptor:**

- Adds `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header automatically
- No need to add token manually in every API call

**Response Interceptor:**

- Handles 401 (unauthorized) globally → logout and redirect
- Prevents duplicated error handling in every component

---

## Routing Architecture

### React Router v7 Setup

**Location:** `src/app/routes/AppRoutes.tsx`

**Why React Router v7?**

- Industry standard
- Code splitting support
- Nested routes
- Type-safe route params
- Excellent TypeScript support

**Route Guards Pattern:**

- `OnlyPrivate` - Protected routes (requires auth)
- `OnlyPublic` - Public routes (redirects if authenticated)

**Why route guards?**

- Centralized auth logic
- Prevents code duplication
- Easy to add new protected routes
- Clear route organization

---

### Sentry Integration

```typescript
const sentryCreateBrowserRouter =
  Sentry.wrapCreateBrowserRouterV6(createBrowserRouter);
```

**Why wrap router?**

- Automatic error tracking on route changes
- Performance monitoring per route
- User session recording
- Breadcrumb trail for debugging

---

### Path Constants

**Location:** `src/shared/constants/appPaths.ts`

**Why `AppPath` class?**

- **Type-Safe Parameters:** Compile-time checks for required params
- **Single Source of Truth:** Change path once
- **Easy Refactoring:** Find all usages
- **Prevents Typos:** No hardcoded strings

**Pattern:**

```typescript
export const appPaths = {
  BUDGET: new AppPath('/budgets/:budgetId'),
};
```

See [PATTERNS.md#6-routing-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#6-routing-pattern) for usage examples.

---

## Repository Pattern (Data Layer)

**Purpose:** Encapsulate API calls and React Query logic.

**Why repository pattern?**

- **Separation of Concerns:** UI doesn't know about API details
- **Reusability:** One hook used by many components
- **Testability:** Mock repositories instead of API
- **Type Safety:** Typed responses
- **Consistency:** Standard naming and error handling

**Naming Convention:**

- `useLoadX` - Queries (read)
- `useCreateX` - Mutations (create)
- `useEditX` - Mutations (update)
- `useDeleteX` - Mutations (delete)

**Why this naming?**

- Clear intent (load vs create vs edit)
- Consistent across codebase
- Easy to search
- Aligns with HTTP methods

See [PATTERNS.md#4-data-fetching-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#4-data-fetching-pattern) for implementation examples.

---

## Modal & Sidebar Architecture

### Service-Managed UI Pattern

**Concept:** UI state (modals, sidebars) managed by services, not component state.

**Why service-managed?**

- **Open from Anywhere:** Even outside React (services, utilities)
- **Centralized State:** One source of truth
- **Type Safety:** Props validated at compile time
- **Animation Support:** State-driven animations
- **Stacking Support:** Multiple sidebars open simultaneously

**Why not component state?**

- Can't open modal from service/utility
- Hard to share state between components
- Each modal needs own state management
- No type safety for props

**Type Safety via Mapped Types:**

```typescript
export type SidebarPropsT = {
  [SidebarNameE.EDIT_BUDGET]: { budgetId: UuidT };
  [SidebarNameE.CREATE_TRANSACTION]: void;
};
```

TypeScript ensures correct props for each sidebar at compile time.

See [PATTERNS.md#1-modal-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#1-modal-pattern) and [PATTERNS.md#2-sidebar-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#2-sidebar-pattern) for usage examples.

---

## Error Handling Architecture

### Multi-Layer Error Handling

**Philosophy:** Handle errors at the appropriate layer, fail gracefully.

**Layers:**

1. **Global Error Boundary** - Catches uncaught errors, shows fallback UI
2. **Route Error Boundary** - Catches routing errors, shows error page
3. **React Query** - Throws errors to boundaries (`throwOnError: true`)
4. **Form Validation** - Field-level errors, prevent submission
5. **Service Layer** - Transform errors, user-friendly messages

**Why multi-layer?**

- Different error types need different handling
- User sees appropriate feedback
- Errors don't crash entire app
- All errors tracked in Sentry

**Error Flow:**

```
API Error
  ↓
Service catches & transforms
  ↓
Repository re-throws
  ↓
Component catches OR Error Boundary catches
  ↓
Display to user (notification or error page)
  ↓
Report to Sentry (if uncaught)
```

---

## Authentication Architecture

### Flow

```
1. User enters email → POST /api/auth/login
   ↓
2. Server sends 6-digit code to email
   ↓
3. User enters code → POST /api/auth/verify
   ↓
4. Server returns JWT token
   ↓
5. authService.login(token)
   ├─ Store in localStorage
   ├─ Update global state
   └─ Set Sentry user
   ↓
6. Navigate to app
   ↓
7. Axios interceptor adds token to all requests
   ↓
8. On 401 error → authService.logout() → Navigate to /login
```

**Why JWT?**

- Stateless (no server-side session storage)
- Can be validated independently
- Contains user info (for Sentry tracking)
- Industry standard

**Why localStorage?**

- Persists across browser sessions
- Simple API
- Synchronous (no async needed)

**Why not cookies?**

- More complex (CSRF protection needed)
- Size limitations
- Cookie policy restrictions

**Token Injection:**

- Axios request interceptor adds token automatically
- No need to add manually in every request

**Token Invalidation:**

- On 401 response, axios interceptor triggers logout
- Clears localStorage, global state, React Query cache
- Redirects to login page

---

## Form Handling Architecture

### AuForm Component

**Why custom form wrapper?**

- **Consistent API:** All forms work the same
- **Server Validation:** Automatic error mapping
- **Auto-Focus:** First error field gets focus
- **Enter Key Handling:** Submit on Enter (last input)
- **Error Display:** Standardized error UI

**Validation Error Flow:**

```
1. Form submits → API call
   ↓
2. Server returns 400 with ValidationError
   ↓
3. AuForm catches ValidationError
   ↓
4. Maps field errors to form fields
   ↓
5. Displays errors below inputs
   ↓
6. Focuses first error field
```

**Why this approach?**

- Client and server validation aligned
- User sees errors next to relevant field
- Keyboard navigation works (auto-focus)
- Consistent UX across all forms

---

## Performance Optimizations

### 1. Code Splitting

- **Route-based:** Each page lazy loaded
- **Dynamic imports:** Heavy components on demand
- **Vite handles:** Automatic chunk splitting

### 2. React Query Caching

- **5-minute stale time:** Avoid redundant requests
- **Background refetching:** Always fresh data
- **Optimistic updates:** Instant UI feedback

### 3. Signals (Fine-Grained Reactivity)

- **Only subscribed components re-render**
- **No Context provider overhead**
- **Minimal wasted renders**

### 4. Form Optimization

- **Uncontrolled inputs:** No re-render on keystroke
- **Validation on blur:** Not on every change
- **Debounced validation:** For async checks

### 5. Memoization

- **React.memo:** For expensive components
- **useMemo:** For expensive calculations
- **useCallback:** For stable function references

---

## Development Tools

### Vite Configuration

**Why Vite over Create React App?**

- **Faster:** ES modules, no bundling in dev
- **Smaller:** Optimized production builds
- **Modern:** Built for modern browsers
- **Flexible:** Easy to configure

**Features:**

- HMR (Hot Module Replacement)
- Fast refresh for React
- Proxy to backend at `/api`
- Tree-shaking and minification
- Source maps for Sentry

### TypeScript Configuration

**Strict Mode Enabled:**

- Catches more errors at compile time
- Forces explicit types
- Prevents `any` proliferation

**Path Aliases:**

- Configured in `tsconfig.json` and `vite.config.ts`
- Must be kept in sync
- Makes imports cleaner

See [PATTERNS.md#7-path-aliases](./PATTERNS.md#7-path-aliases) for details.

### Sentry Integration

**Why Sentry?**

- **Error Tracking:** Catch production errors
- **Source Maps:** Readable stack traces
- **User Context:** Know who experienced error
- **Performance Monitoring:** Track slow routes
- **Session Replay:** See what user did before error

**Configuration:**

- DSN from environment variable
- Router instrumentation for navigation tracking
- 10% traces sample rate (balance cost/coverage)

---

## Testing Architecture

### Why These Tools?

**Vitest over Jest:**

- Faster (native ESM support)
- Better Vite integration
- Compatible with Jest API
- Modern, actively maintained

**@testing-library/react:**

- Test user behavior, not implementation
- Encourages accessible components
- Query by role, label, text (not class names)
- Industry standard

**Playwright over Cypress:**

- True cross-browser testing
- Better TypeScript support
- Faster execution
- Built-in auto-waiting

### What to Test Where

**Unit Tests (Vitest):**

- Business logic in services
- Complex calculations
- Validation functions
- Edge cases

**Component Tests (@testing-library):**

- User interactions
- Conditional rendering
- Form validation
- Error states

**E2E Tests (Playwright):**

- Critical user flows
- Multi-page journeys
- Real browser behavior
- Integration between features

**What NOT to Test:**

- Simple prop passing
- Framework behavior
- Trivial assignments
- Third-party libraries

---

## Key Architectural Decisions

### Why @preact/signals over Context API?

✅ Fine-grained updates, no provider overhead, tiny bundle

### Why React Query over Redux?

✅ Purpose-built for server state, less boilerplate, auto caching

### Why react-hook-form over Formik?

✅ Better performance (uncontrolled), smaller bundle, better TypeScript

### Why Axios over Fetch?

✅ Interceptors, timeouts, automatic error handling, better browser support

### Why SCSS over CSS-in-JS?

✅ No runtime cost, standard CSS syntax, better tooling, clear separation

### Why Service-Managed UI (modals/sidebars)?

✅ Type-safe props, open from anywhere, animation support, centralized state

### Why Repository Pattern?

✅ Separation of concerns, reusable, testable, consistent API

### Why Route Guards?

✅ Centralized auth logic, no duplication, clear route organization

---

## Future Architecture Considerations

### Potential Improvements

1. **State Management Evolution:**

   - Monitor signals performance at scale
   - Consider Zustand if complexity grows

2. **Data Fetching:**

   - Explore GraphQL for flexible querying
   - WebSockets/SSE for real-time updates

3. **Performance:**

   - Service Workers for offline support
   - Web Workers for heavy computations
   - Virtual scrolling for large lists

4. **Modularity:**

   - Micro-frontends if team scales
   - Feature flags for gradual rollouts

5. **Monitoring:**
   - Real User Monitoring (RUM)
   - Web Vitals tracking
   - Custom performance metrics

---

## For Agents

When making architectural decisions:

1. **Follow established patterns** - Don't introduce new approaches without reason
2. **Check this doc first** - Understand why choices were made
3. **Consistency over cleverness** - Use existing solutions
4. **Performance matters** - Consider bundle size and runtime cost
5. **Type safety is key** - Leverage TypeScript fully

If you need to deviate from established architecture, document why in your output.

825→ [CookieNameE.PREFERENCES]: PreferencesT; 826→}; 827→``` 828→ 829→**Usage Example:** 830→ 831→```typescript 832→// Remember user preference 833→cookieService.set(CookieNameE.LANGUAGE, 'en', 365); 834→ 835→// Read on page load 836→const language = cookieService.get(CookieNameE.LANGUAGE) || 'en'; 837→ 838→// Clear on logout 839→cookieService.remove(CookieNameE.SESSION_ID); 840→``` 841→ 842→**Dependencies:** `universal-cookie` 843→ 844→--- 845→ 846→## Service Patterns 847→ 848→### Pattern 1: Global State Services 849→ 850→Services that manage global state using signals. 851→ 852→**Services:** `authService`, `modalService`, `sidebarService`, `notificationService` 853→ 854→**Pattern:** 855→ 856→```typescript 857→import { createGlobalValueSetter } from 'Shared/store/createGlobalValueSetter'; 865→ }, 866→ remove: id => { 867→ updateField(current => current.filter(i => i.id !== id)); 868→ }, 869→}; 870→``` 871→ 872→**Why:** 873→ 874→- Centralized state management 875→- Type-safe updates 876→- Reactive - components re-render automatically 877→- Service logic separated from UI 878→ 879→--- 880→ 881→### Pattern 2: Storage Services 882→ 883→Services that wrap browser storage APIs with type safety. 884→ 885→**Services:** `localStorageService`, `cookieService` 886→ 887→**Pattern:** 888→ 889→```typescript 890→export const storageService = { 891→ set(name: T, value: TypeMap[T]) { 892→ // Type-safe set 893→ }, 894→ get(name: T): TypeMap[T] | null { 895→ // Type-safe get with return type inference 896→ }, 897→ remove(name: T) { 898→ // Type-safe remove 899→ }, 900→}; 901→``` 902→ 903→**Benefits:** 904→ 905→- TypeScript ensures correct types for each key 906→- Centralized storage logic 907→- Easy to add new storage keys 908→- Consistent error handling 909→ 910→--- 911→ 912→### Pattern 3: API Services 913→ 914→Services that wrap API calls with error handling. 915→ 916→**Services:** `aiAssistantService` 917→ 918→**Pattern:** 919→ 920→```typescript 921→export const apiService = { 922→ async operation(params): Promise { 923→ try { 924→ const response = await axiosClient.post(endpoint, params); 925→ return response.data; 926→ } catch (error) { 927→ if (error instanceof AxiosError) { 928→ // Handle specific errors 929→ if (error.code === 'ECONNABORTED') { 930→ throw new Error('User-friendly message'); 931→ } 932→ } 933→ throw error; // Re-throw unknown errors 934→ } 935→ }, 936→}; 937→``` 938→ 939→**Benefits:** 940→ 941→- Consistent error handling 942→- User-friendly error messages 943→- Timeout handling 944→- Type-safe responses 945→ 946→--- 947→ 948→## Error Handling 949→ 950→### General Principles 951→ 952→1. **Catch at service level** - Services should catch and transform errors 953→2. **User-friendly messages** - Convert technical errors to readable messages 954→3. **Log for debugging** - Console errors in development 955→4. **Graceful degradation** - Return sensible defaults when possible 956→ 957→### Examples 958→ 959→**Storage Services:** 960→ 961→```typescript 962→try { 963→ return JSON.parse(data); 964→} catch { 965→ // Remove corrupted data and return null 966→ localStorage.removeItem(key); 967→ return null; 968→} 969→``` 970→ 971→**API Services:** 972→ 973→```typescript 974→catch (error: unknown) { 975→ if (error instanceof AxiosError) { 976→ if (error.code === 'ECONNABORTED') { 977→ throw new Error('Request timed out'); 978→ } 979→ if (error.response?.status === 500) { 980→ throw new Error('Server error'); 981→ } 982→ } 983→ throw error; // Unknown errors re-thrown 984→} 985→``` 986→ 987→**Notification:** 988→ 989→```typescript 990→try { 991→ await riskyOperation(); 992→ notificationService.addAlert('Success!'); 993→} catch (error) { 994→ notificationService.addErrorAlert(error.message); 995→} 996→``` 997→ 998→--- 999→ 1000→## Testing Services 1001→ 1002→Services should have tests in `__tests__` directories: 1003→ 1004→``` 1005→src/services/ 1006→ ├── authService.ts 1007→ ├── authService.test.ts 1008→ ├── modalService.ts 1009→ └── modalService.test.ts 1010→``` 1011→ 1012→**Test Guidelines:** 1013→ 1014→- Test each public method 1015→- Test error cases 1016→- Mock dependencies (storage, API calls) 1017→- Test state changes for state-management services 1018→ 1019→**Example:** 1020→ 1021→```typescript 1022→describe('authService', () => { 1023→ it('should initialize with token from localStorage', () => { 1024→ localStorageService.set(LocalStorageNameE.ACCESS_TOKEN, 'token-123'); 1025→ 1026→ authService.initialize(); 1027→ 1028→ expect(globalStore[GlobalStoreFieldE.AUTH_TOKEN].value).toBe('token-123'); 1029→ }); 1030→ 1031→ it('should clear cache on logout', () => { 1032→ authService.logout(); 1033→ 1034→ expect(queryClient.clear).toHaveBeenCalled(); 1035→ }); 1036→}); 1037→``` 1038→ 1039→--- 1040→ 1041→## Request Client Utilities 1042→ 1043→### errorInterceptor 1044→ 1045→**Location:** `src/shared/requestClient/errorInterceptor.ts` 1046→ 1047→**Purpose:** Global API error handling via axios interceptor. Displays user-friendly error messages in a modal for all API errors not handled by ValidationError or AuthorizationError. 1048→ 1049→**Exports:** 1050→ 1051→#### `handleErrorModal(error: AxiosError)` 1052→ 1053→Opens an error modal with a user-friendly message extracted from the API response. 1054→ 1055→```typescript 1056→import { handleErrorModal } from 'Shared/requestClient/errorInterceptor'; 1057→ 1058→// Called automatically by axios interceptor 1059→handleErrorModal(error); 1060→``` 1061→ 1062→**Message Extraction Logic:** 1063→ 1064→1. First tries `response.data.message` (string) 1065→2. Falls back to `response.data.errors[0].msg` (validation error format) 1066→3. Falls back to `'Something went wrong. Please try again.'` 1067→ 1068→**Integration:** 1069→ 1070→The interceptor is integrated in `axiosClient.ts` after ValidationError (400) and AuthorizationError (401) handlers: 1071→ 1072→```typescript 1073→// In axiosClient.ts response interceptor 1074→axiosClient.interceptors.response.use( 1075→ response => response, 1076→ error => { 1077→ // Existing ValidationError handling (400 with errors array) 1078→ // Existing AuthorizationError handling (401) 1079→ 1080→ // NEW: Handle all other API errors with modal 1081→ handleErrorModal(error); 1082→ 1083→ return Promise.reject(error); 1084→ }, 1085→); 1086→``` 1087→ 1088→**Errors NOT Handled:** 1089→ 1090→- ValidationError (400 with validation errors) - handled by form fields 1091→- AuthorizationError (401) - triggers logout, no modal 1092→- Network errors during critical auth operations 1093→ 1094→**Dependencies:** `modalService`, `ModalNameE` 1095→ 1096→**Tests:** 1097→ 1098→- Unit: `errorInterceptor.test.ts` (9 tests) 1099→ 1100→--- 1101→ 1102→## Service Dependencies 1103→ 1104→``` 1105→authService 1106→ ├── localStorageService 1107→ ├── globalStore 1108→ ├── queryClient 1109→ └── sentryHelpers 1110→ 1111→modalService 1112→ └── globalStore 1113→ 1114→sidebarService 1115→ └── globalStore 1116→ 1117→notificationService 1118→ └── globalStore 1119→ 1120→scrollLockService 1121→ └── (none - pure DOM) 1122→ 1123→aiAssistantService 1124→ └── axiosClient 1125→ 1126→aiConversationStorageService 1127→ └── localStorageService 1128→ 1129→localStorageService 1130→ └── jsonHelpers 1131→ 1132→cookieService 1133→ └── universal-cookie (external) 1134→``` 1135→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. ## 8. API Error Handling Pattern

### How It Works

API errors are handled globally through axios response interceptors. Different error types are handled differently:

1. **ValidationError (400)** - Form field validation errors shown inline
2. **AuthorizationError (401)** - Triggers logout, redirects to login
3. **Other API Errors** - Display in error modal with user-friendly message

**Implementation:**

- Global interceptor in `axiosClient.ts` catches all API errors
- `errorInterceptor.ts` handles displaying error modal
- ErrorModal component renders globally in App.tsx
- modalService manages error modal state

### When to Use

- All API errors that should notify the user
- Server errors (500)
- Network errors
- Any error not handled by ValidationError or AuthorizationError

### Usage Example

```typescript
// Automatic - errors are caught globally by axios interceptor
// No manual error handling needed for API errors

const handleSave = async () => {
  // If this fails with 500, error modal appears automatically
  await requestClient.post(ApiGatewayE.BUDGETS, undefined, budgetData);
  notificationService.addAlert('Budget saved!');
};

// Manual - if you need to show a specific error message
import { modalService } from 'Services/modalService';
import { ModalNameE } from 'Components/modal/ModalNameE';

modalService.open(ModalNameE.ERROR, { message: 'Custom error message' });
```

### Error Message Extraction

The interceptor extracts user-friendly messages in this order:

1. `response.data.message` (string)
2. `response.data.errors[0].msg` (validation format)
3. Fallback: `'Something went wrong. Please try again.'`

### Example Files

- Error interceptor: `src/shared/requestClient/errorInterceptor.ts`
- Axios client: `src/shared/requestClient/axiosClient.ts`
- Error modal component: `src/components/modal/errorModal/ErrorModal.tsx`
- Error modal styles: `src/components/modal/errorModal/errorModal.css`

---

State is defined in `globalStore` and accessed/updated through utilities. 120→ 121→**Core Components:** 122→ 123→- `globalStore` - Central store with signals for each field 124→- `useGlobalValue(field)` - Hook to read state in components 125→- `createGlobalValueSetter(field)` - Creates updater function for services 126→ 127→**Implementation:** 128→ 129→- Each field is a `signal` that can be subscribed to 130→- React components subscribe using `useGlobalValue` 131→- Services update state using setter functions 132→- Changes automatically trigger re-renders in subscribed components 133→ 134→### When to Use 135→ 136→- Auth token 137→- Notifications 138→- Modals and sidebars state 139→- IDs lists (e.g., deleted items for optimistic updates) 140→- Any state shared across multiple components 141→ 142→### Usage Example 143→ 144→```typescript 145→// In a service 146→import { createGlobalValueSetter } from 'Shared/store/createGlobalValueSetter'; 147→import { GlobalStoreFieldE } from 'Shared/types/GlobalStoreFieldE'; 148→ 149→const updateNotifications = createGlobalValueSetter( 150→ GlobalStoreFieldE.NOTIFICATIONS, 151→); 152→ 153→export const notificationService = { 154→ add: notification => { 155→ updateNotifications(current => [...current, notification]); 156→ }, 157→}; 158→ 159→// In a component 160→import { useGlobalValue } from 'Shared/store/useGlobalValue'; 161→import { GlobalStoreFieldE } from 'Shared/types/GlobalStoreFieldE'; 162→ 163→const MyComponent = () => { 164→ const notifications = useGlobalValue(GlobalStoreFieldE.NOTIFICATIONS); 165→ 166→ return
{notifications.length} notifications

Each repository hook handles a specific operation. 185→ 186→**Naming Convention:** 187→ 188→- `useLoadX` - Fetch single item or list 189→- `useCreateX` - Create new item 190→- `useEditX` - Update existing item 191→- `useDeleteX` - Delete item 192→ 193→**Implementation:** 194→ 195→- All API calls go through `requestClient` (Axios wrapper) 196→- Hooks use `useQuery` (read) or `useMutation` (write) 197→- Query keys based on `ApiGatewayE` enum 198→- Automatic cache invalidation after mutations 199→- Infinite queries for paginated lists 200→ 201→### When to Use 202→ 203→- All API interactions 204→- Server state management 205→- Caching and background updates 206→- Optimistic updates 207→ 208→### Query Hook Example (Read) 209→ 210→```typescript 211→import { useInfiniteQuery } from '@tanstack/react-query'; 212→import { requestClient } from 'Shared/requestClient/requestClient'; 213→import { ApiGatewayE } from 'Shared/types/api/ApiGatewayE'; 214→ 215→export const useTransactionList = filters => { 216→ return useInfiniteQuery({ 217→ queryKey: [ApiGatewayE.BANK_TRANSACTIONS, filters], 218→ queryFn: async ({ pageParam = 1 }) => { 219→ return await requestClient.get(ApiGatewayE.BANK_TRANSACTIONS, undefined, { 220→ page: pageParam, 221→ ...filters, 222→ }); 223→ }, 224→ getNextPageParam: lastPage => { 225→ return lastPage.page < lastPage.totalPages ? lastPage.page + 1 : undefined; 226→ }, 227→ }); 228→}; 229→ 230→// In component - destructure what you need 231→const MyComponent = () => { 232→ const { data, isLoading, fetchNextPage, hasNextPage } = 233→ useTransactionList(filters); 234→ 235→ const transactions = data?.pages.flatMap(p => p.results) ?? Different error types are handled differently: 505→ 506→1. **ValidationError (400)** - Form field validation errors shown inline 507→2. **AuthorizationError (401)** - Triggers logout, redirects to login 508→3. **Other API Errors** - Display in error modal with user-friendly message 509→ 510→**Implementation:** 511→ 512→- Global interceptor in `axiosClient.ts` catches all API errors 513→- `errorInterceptor.ts` handles displaying error modal 514→- ErrorModal component renders globally in App.tsx 515→- modalService manages error modal state 516→ 517→### When to Use 518→ 519→- All API errors that should notify the user 520→- Server errors (500) 521→- Network errors 522→- Any error not handled by ValidationError or AuthorizationError 523→ 524→### Usage Example 525→ 526→```typescript 527→// Automatic - errors are caught globally by axios interceptor 528→// No manual error handling needed for API errors 529→ 530→const handleSave = async () => { 531→ // If this fails with 500, error modal appears automatically 532→ await requestClient.post(ApiGatewayE.BUDGETS, undefined, budgetData); 533→ notificationService.addAlert('Budget saved!'); 534→}; 535→ 536→// Manual - if you need to show a specific error message 537→import { modalService } from 'Services/modalService'; 538→import { ModalNameE } from 'Components/modal/ModalNameE'; 539→ 540→modalService.open(ModalNameE.ERROR, { message: 'Custom error message' }); 541→``` 542→ 543→### Error Message Extraction 544→ 545→The interceptor extracts user-friendly messages in this order: 546→ 547→1. `response.data.message` (string) 548→2. `response.data.errors[0].msg` (validation format) 549→3. Fallback: `'Something went wrong. Please try again.'` 550→ 551→### Example Files 552→ 553→- Error interceptor: `src/shared/requestClient/errorInterceptor.ts` 554→- Axios client: `src/shared/requestClient/axiosClient.ts` 555→- Error modal component: `src/components/modal/errorModal/ErrorModal.tsx` 556→- Error modal styles: `src/components/modal/errorModal/errorModal.css` 557→ 558→--- 559→ 560→## 9. Theme System Pattern 561→ 562→### How It Works 563→ 564→The application automatically detects and applies theme colors based on the user's browser or operating system preference via the CSS media query `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)`. 565→ 566→**Implementation:** 567→ 568→- Theme variables defined in `src/app/styles/variables.scss` 569→- Light theme: Default CSS variables at `:root` 570→- Dark theme: Overridden variables in `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) :root` 571→- Semantic color variables used throughout all components 572→- No manual theme toggle - theme switches automatically with OS/browser settings 573→ 574→### Semantic Color Variables 575→ 576→All components use semantic variables instead of specific colors. This ensures theme consistency. 577→ 578→**Light Theme Semantic Variables:** 579→ 580→```scss 581→--color-background: var(--color-primary-blue-light); 582→--color-foreground: #fff; 583→--color-text: #000; 584→--color-text-subtle: var(--color-gray-2); 585→--color-text-muted: var(--color-gray-1); 586→--color-border: var(--color-gray-4); 587→--color-border-subtle: var(--color-gray-3); 588→--color-placeholder: var(--color-gray-3); 589→--color-disabled-bg: var(--color-gray-4); 590→--color-disabled-text: var(--color-gray-3); 591→``` 592→ 593→**Dark Theme Semantic Variables:** 594→ 595→```scss 596→@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) { 597→ :root { 598→ --color-background: #0f1419; 599→ --color-foreground: #1a1f26; 600→ --color-text: #e0e0e0; 601→ --color-text-subtle: var(--color-gray-5); 602→ --color-text-muted: var(--color-gray-6); 603→ --color-border: var(--color-gray-7); 604→ --color-border-subtle: var(--color-gray-8); 605→ --color-placeholder: var(--color-gray-8); 606→ --color-disabled-bg: var(--color-gray-7); 607→ --color-disabled-text: var(--color-gray-6); 608→ } 609→} 610→``` 611→ 612→### Brand Color Adjustments 613→ 614→Brand blue colors are softened in dark mode for better contrast and readability: 615→ 616→**Light Mode:** 617→- `--color-primary-blue: #075eff` (bright, saturated blue) 618→- `--color-primary-blue-darker: #0640ac` 619→- `--color-primary-blue-light: #f3f7ff` 620→ 621→**Dark Mode:** 622→- `--color-primary-blue: #5a95ff` (softer, less saturated) 623→- `--color-primary-blue-darker: #7eabff` (lighter for contrast) 624→- `--color-primary-blue-light: #1a2a3d` (dark background instead of light) 625→ 626→### When to Use 627→ 628→- Use semantic variables for all color styling (never hardcoded colors) 629→- Use specific colors only for brand colors in variables.scss 630→- All SCSS files should reference semantic variables 631→ 632→### Usage Example 633→ 634→```scss 635→// SCSS file - use semantic variables only 636→.button { 637→ background-color: var(--color-button-primary); 638→ color: var(--color-button-text-on-primary); 639→ border: 1px solid var(--color-border); 640→ 641→ &:hover { 642→ background-color: var(--color-button-primary-hover); 643→ } 644→ 645→ &:disabled { 646→ background-color: var(--color-disabled-bg); 647→ color: var(--color-disabled-text); 648→ } 649→} 650→ 651→.text-subtle { 652→ color: var(--color-text-subtle); 653→} 654→ 655→.input { 656→ background-color: var(--color-foreground); 657→ color: var(--color-text); 658→ border: 1px solid var(--color-input-border); 659→ 660→ &::placeholder { 661→ color: var(--color-placeholder); 662→ } 663→} 664→``` 665→ 666→### Theme Switching 667→ 668→Theme switching happens automatically - no code needed: 669→ 670→1. User changes OS/browser theme preference 671→2. Browser applies matching `prefers-color-scheme` media query 672→3. CSS variables automatically update 673→4. All components re-render with new colors 674→ 675→**No manual theme toggle or localStorage required.** 676→ 677→### Example Files 678→ 679→- Variables: `src/app/styles/variables.scss` 680→- Helpers: `src/app/styles/helpers.scss` 681→- Component example: `src/components/form/button/auButton.scss` 682→ 683→--- 684→ 685→## Summary 686→ 687→### Key Principles 688→ 689→1. **Services manage UI state** - Use modalService, sidebarService for modals/sidebars 690→2. **Type-safe props** - Enums + mapped types ensure correct props at compile time 691→3. **Repository pattern** - All API calls go through repository hooks 692→4. **Absolute imports** - Always use path aliases 693→5. **Naming conventions** - Au* for forms, *Page for pages, \*Sidebar for sidebars 694→6. **Route guards** - Protect routes with OnlyPrivate/OnlyPublic 695→ 696→--- 697→ 698→## Common Workflows 699→ 700→### Opening a sidebar with form 701→ 702→```typescript 703→import { sidebarService } from 'Services/sidebarService'; 704→import { SidebarNameE } from 'Components/sidebar/types/SidebarNameE'; 705→ 706→// Clear stack and open 707→sidebarService.openFirst(SidebarNameE.CREATE_BUDGET, { budgetId }); 708→ 709→// Add to existing stack 710→sidebarService.open(SidebarNameE.EDIT_BUDGET_ITEM, { itemId }); 711→``` 712→ 713→### Fetching and creating data 714→ 715→```typescript 716→import { useLoadBudgets } from 'Repositories/budgets/useLoadBudgets'; 717→import { useCreateBudget } from 'Repositories/budgets/useCreateBudget'; 718→ 719→const MyComponent = () => { 720→ const { data: budgets, isLoading } = useLoadBudgets(); 721→ const createBudget = useCreateBudget(); 722→ 723→ const handleCreate = async () => { 724→ await createBudget({ name: 'My Budget', startDate: new Date() }); 725→ }; 726→ 727→ return ( 728→ <> 729→ {isLoading && } 730→ {budgets?.map(budget => ( 731→ 732→ ))} 733→ New Budget 734→ 735→ ); 736→}; 737→``` 738→ 739→### Reading/updating global state 740→ 741→```typescript 742→import { useGlobalValue } from 'Shared/store/useGlobalValue'; 743→import { createGlobalValueSetter } from 'Shared/store/createGlobalValueSetter'; 744→import { GlobalStoreFieldE } from 'Shared/types/GlobalStoreFieldE'; 745→ 746→// In component (read) 747→const MyComponent = () => { 748→ const sidebars = useGlobalValue(GlobalStoreFieldE.SIDEBAR); 749→ return
{sidebars.length} sidebars open
; 750→}; 751→ 752→// In service (update) 753→const updateSidebar = createGlobalValueSetter(GlobalStoreFieldE.SIDEBAR); 754→ 755→export const sidebarService = { 756→ open: sidebar => { 757→ updateSidebar(current => [...current, sidebar]); 758→ }, 759→}; 760→``` 761→ 762→### Navigating to route 763→ 764→```typescript 765→import { useNavigate } from 'react-router-dom'; 766→import { appPaths } from 'Shared/constants/appPaths'; 767→ 768→const MyComponent = () => { 769→ const navigate = useNavigate(); 770→ 771→ const goToTransactions = () => { 772→ navigate(appPaths.TRANSACTIONS.getPath()); 773→ }; 774→ 775→ const goToBudget = budgetId => { 776→ navigate(appPaths.BUDGET.getPath({ budgetId })); 777→ }; 778→ 779→ return ( 780→ <> 781→ 782→ 783→ 784→ ); 785→}; 786→``` 787→ 788→### Form with validation 789→ 790→```typescript 791→import { FormProvider, useForm } from 'react-hook-form'; 792→import AuField from 'Components/form/field/AuField'; 793→import AuButton from 'Components/form/button/AuButton'; 794→import { validationPatterns } from 'Shared/constants/validationPatterns'; 795→import { replacePatterns } from 'Shared/constants/replacePatterns'; 796→ 797→const MyForm = () => { 798→ const formMethods = useForm(); 799→ 800→ const handleSubmit = async data => { 801→ try { 802→ await saveData(data); 803→ notificationService.addAlert('Saved!'); 804→ } catch (error) { 805→ notificationService.addErrorAlert(error.message); 806→ } 807→ }; 808→ 809→ return ( 810→ 811→

828→ 829→ ); 830→}; 831→``` 832→ 833→--- 834→ 835→## For Agents 836→ 837→When implementing features: 838→ 839→1. **Check this doc first** - Find the relevant pattern 840→2. **Copy working examples** - Use code from this guide 841→3. **Follow naming conventions** - Consistency is key 842→4. **Use path aliases** - Never relative imports 843→5. **Test the pattern** - Ensure it works before moving on 844→ 845→If you need to understand _why_ a pattern exists, see [ARCHITECTURE.md](./ARCHITECTURE.md). 846→

## Component Naming Conventions

### Form Components

- **Always** prefix with `Au`
- PascalCase: `AuButton`, `AuField`, `AuSelect`
- Located in `src/components/form/`

### Page Components

- Suffix with `Page`
- Example: `TransactionsPage`, `BudgetPage`
- Located in `src/pages/`

### Sidebar Components

- Suffix with `Sidebar`
- Example: `EditBudgetSidebar`, `TransactionFormSidebar`
- Located in page directories: `src/pages/budget/sidebars/`

### Layout Components

- Suffix with `Layout`
- Example: `MainLayout`, `BaseLayout`
- Located in `src/components/layouts/`

### SVG Components

- Suffix with `Svg` or `SVG`
- Example: `PencilSvg`, `CloseSvg`
- Located in `src/components/svg/`

---

automatic theme support. 718→ 719→**Key Principles:** 720→ 721→1. **Semantic Variables Only** - Use `var(--color-text)`, not hardcoded colors 722→2. **No `!important`** - CSS variables inherit naturally 723→3. **No Manual Theme Logic** - No `@media (prefers-color-scheme)` in component files 724→4. **Light Theme First** - Default values at `:root`, overrides in media query 725→ 726→### Semantic Variables Available 727→ 728→All components have access to these variables: 729→ 730→```scss 731→// Text colors 732→--color-text // Primary text (black in light, light gray in dark) 733→--color-text-subtle // Secondary text (gray-2 in light, gray-5 in dark) 734→--color-text-muted // Tertiary text (gray-1 in light, gray-6 in dark) 735→ 736→// Backgrounds 737→--color-background // (gray-4 in light, gray-7 in dark) 742→--color-border-subtle // Secondary border (gray-3 in light, gray-8 in dark) 743→ 744→// Interactive elements 745→--color-button-primary // Button background 746→--color-button-primary-hover // Button hover 747→--color-button-primary-active // Button active/pressed 748→--color-button-text-on-primary // Text on dark button background 749→--color-input-background // Input field background 750→--color-input-border // Input field border 751→--color-input-border-hover // Input on hover 752→--color-input-border-active // Input on focus 753→--color-placeholder // Placeholder text 754→ 755→// States 756→--color-disabled-bg // Disabled element background 757→--color-disabled-text // Disabled element text 758→--color-checkbox-unchecked // Checkbox unchecked state 759→--color-avatar-placeholder // Avatar placeholder color 760→ 761→// Links 762→--color-link // Link text 763→--color-link-hover // Link on hover 764→``` 765→ 766→See [PATTERNS.md#9-theme-system-pattern](./PATTERNS.md#9-theme-system-pattern) for complete list. 767→ 768→### Example: Styling a Component 769→ 770→```scss 771→// src/components/form/button/auButton.scss 772→.button { 773→ // Always use semantic variables 774→ background-color: var(--color-button-primary); 775→ color: var(--color-button-text-on-primary); 776→ border: none; 777→ 778→ &:hover { 779→ background-color: var(--color-button-primary-hover); 780→ } 781→ 782→ &:active { 783→ background-color: var(--color-button-primary-active); 784→ } 785→ 786→ &:disabled { 787→ background-color: var(--color-disabled-bg); 788→ color: var(--color-disabled-text); 789→ cursor: not-allowed; 790→ } 791→} 792→``` 793→ 794→### Theme Behavior 795→ 796→**Automatic theme switching:** 797→ 798→1. Component uses semantic variables: `background-color: var(--color-button-primary)` 799→2. Light mode (default): `--color-button-primary: #075eff` (bright blue) 800→3. Dark mode (OS preference): `--color-button-primary: #5a95ff` (softer blue) 801→4. No component code changes needed - CSS variables handle it! 802→ 803→**Browser updates colors automatically when:** 804→ 805→- User changes OS theme preference 806→- User changes browser theme setting 807→- No page refresh required - instant update 808→ 809→### What NOT to Do 810→ 811→```scss 812→// ❌ NEVER use hardcoded colors 813→.button { 814→ background-color: #075eff; // WRONG - won't adapt to dark theme 815→} 816→ 817→// ❌ NEVER use specific color variables 818→.button { 819→ background-color: var(--color-primary-blue); // WRONG - use semantic instead 820→} 821→ 822→// ❌ NEVER add @media (prefers-color-scheme) in components 823→// This is handled globally in src/app/styles/variables.scss 824→.button { 825→ @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) { 826→ background-color: #5a95ff; // WRONG - don't do this 827→ } 828→} 829→ 830→// ❌ NEVER mix hardcoded colors with variables 831→.button { 832→ background-color: var(--color-button-primary); 833→ border: 1px solid #cccccc; // WRONG - should use var(--color-border) 834→} 835→``` 836→ 837→--- 838→ 839→## Component Patterns 840→ 841→### 1.
